title: Neurosurgical Management of Chronic Pain
subtitle: Block D — Functional Neurosurgery: Spinal Cord Stimulation, Intrathecal Drug Delivery, Ablative Procedures
guidelines_version: IASP Pain Taxonomy 2020 · NICE NG59 Low Back Pain 2016 · NICE TA159 SCS 2008 · ISAS Guidelines 2020
textbook_refs: Kumar K et al. Spinal Cord Stimulation versus Conventional Medical Management for Neuropathic Pain. Pain 2007;132:179-188 (PROCESS trial)
North RB et al. Spinal-Cord Stimulation versus Repeated Lumbosacral Spine Surgery for Chronic Pain. NEJM 2005;353:666-668
Raslan AM. Percutaneous computed tomography-guided radiofrequency ablation of upper cervical spinal cord pain-conducting tracts. Neurosurgery 2008;62:226-233

learning_objectives: Classify chronic pain by mechanism (nociceptive, neuropathic, nociplastic) and apply this to treatment selection
Identify appropriate candidates for spinal cord stimulation (SCS) and describe the mechanism of paresthesia-based and subperception (HF-SCS, burst) stimulation
Apply the PROCESS trial evidence base for SCS in failed back surgery syndrome (FBSS) and complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS)
Describe the indications and technique for intrathecal drug delivery (IDDS) for chronic cancer and non-cancer pain
Identify the neurosurgical ablative procedures for pain (cordotomy, cingulotomy, rhizotomy) and their specific indications
Recognise the role of patient selection, psychological screening, and multidisciplinary teams in neurosurgical pain management

sections.title: Classification of Chronic Pain and Neurosurgical Indications

blocks.content:
Chronic pain is defined as pain persisting beyond the expected healing period (typically >3 months) or associated with a chronic disease process. The IASP 2020 taxonomy classifies pain by mechanism into three categories: **nociceptive pain** (arising from actual or threatened tissue damage, activating nociceptors — e.g., somatic: arthritis, bone fracture; visceral: organ pain); **neuropathic pain** (caused by a lesion or disease of the somatosensory nervous system — e.g., post-herpetic neuralgia, diabetic neuropathy, FBSS, CRPS, phantom limb pain); and **nociplastic pain** (altered nociception without clear tissue or neural damage — e.g., fibromyalgia, irritable bowel syndrome, widespread chronic pain). This classification is clinically important because neurosurgical interventions — spinal cord stimulation, IDDS, ablative procedures — are primarily indicated for **neuropathic pain** and selected **nociceptive** (cancer) pain syndromes. Nociplastic pain does not have a structural target for surgery and is managed primarily through rehabilitation, psychology, and pharmacology.

Neurosurgical pain interventions can be classified as **neuromodulatory** (reversible — spinal cord stimulation, DBS, intrathecal drug delivery, transcranial stimulation) or **ablative/destructive** (permanent — cordotomy, cingulotomy, dorsal root entry zone [DREZ] lesioning, rhizotomy). The general principle is to attempt reversible neuromodulation before irreversible ablation. Patient selection involves: chronicity (>6 months of treatment failure), mechanism (neuropathic or selected nociceptive), psychological fitness (absence of untreated depression, psychosis, addiction), and technical feasibility (site of pain accessible to the chosen intervention).

The **psychosocial evaluation** is mandatory before any implantable pain device: structured psychological interview, validated instruments (Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory — MMPI; Pain Catastrophising Scale — PCS; Depression screen — PHQ-9; health-related QoL — SF-36). Predictors of poor outcome: untreated major depression, somatisation disorder, current opioid abuse, secondary gain (legal or disability pending), multiple previous pain surgeries, and poor support systems. **Waddell signs** (≥3) predict poor surgical outcome for any pain procedure. The multidisciplinary pain team (neurosurgeon, pain physician, psychologist, physiotherapist) evaluates candidates before device implantation.

rows: FBSS, CRPS, PHN, diabetic neuropathy, phantom limb
IDDS (opioid ± adjuvant); cordotomy for refractory unilateral

blocks.title: Psychological Screening is Mandatory Before Implantation
blocks.content: All SCS and IDDS candidates require formal psychological evaluation. Contraindications to device implantation: untreated major depression (PHQ-9 ≥15); active suicidality; untreated psychosis; active substance abuse (including opioid abuse); active secondary gain (pending litigation, disability claim directly relating to pain area); somatisation disorder. These factors predict poor outcomes and device removal rather than pain relief. Proceed with implantation only after psychological clearance.

sections.title: Spinal Cord Stimulation: Mechanisms and Indications

blocks.content:
**Spinal cord stimulation (SCS)** delivers electrical stimulation to the dorsal columns of the spinal cord via epidurally placed electrode leads, generating paresthesiae (tingling) in the painful dermatomes. The traditional mechanism is based on the **gate control theory (Melzack and Wall, 1965)**: stimulation of large-diameter Aβ fibres in the dorsal columns activates inhibitory interneurons in the substantia gelatinosa (spinal dorsal horn, lamina II), which presynaptically inhibit nociceptive C-fibre and Aδ-fibre signals — 'closing the gate' to pain transmission. Additional mechanisms include: inhibition of wide dynamic range (WDR) neurons in the dorsal horn; release of inhibitory neurotransmitters (GABA, glycine, serotonin) in the dorsal horn; and supraspinal modulation through descending inhibitory pathways.

Modern SCS paradigms include: (1) **Conventional (paresthesia-based) SCS** — the patient must feel paresthesiae overlapping the pain area (frequency 40-100 Hz, pulse width 200-400 μs); (2) **High-frequency SCS (HF-SCS, 10 kHz)** (Nevro SCS, FDA-approved 2015): 10,000 Hz stimulation produces NO paresthesiae (sub-perception), yet achieves equivalent or superior pain relief compared with conventional SCS for back pain specifically — the SENZA-RCT (Kapural et al. Anesthesiology 2015) showed HF10-SCS superior to conventional SCS for back pain; (3) **Burst stimulation** (Abbott Proclaim DRG): patterned bursts of 500 Hz superimposed on 40 Hz — provides paresthesia-free stimulation with equivalence to conventional SCS and potential emotional/affective pain dimension modulation through medial thalamus/anterior cingulate pathways; (4) **Dorsal root ganglion (DRG) stimulation** — lead placed in the epidural foramen targeting the DRG directly; highly specific dermatomal coverage; FDA-approved for CRPS and focal neuropathic pain; superior for foot pain, inguinal pain, and CRPS compared with conventional SCS.

For SCS implantation, a **trial phase** (7-10 days with percutaneous trial leads) is mandatory — patients are implanted with permanent devices only if the trial achieves ≥50% pain reduction. The permanent system consists of a pulse generator (IPG — rechargeable or non-rechargeable) implanted subcutaneously (typically infraclavicular or flank), connected via extension leads to the spinal electrode.

blocks.title: Gate Control Theory and Its Limits
blocks.content: The gate control theory (Melzack and Wall, Science 1965) postulates that stimulation of non-nociceptive large-diameter (Aβ) fibres activates spinal inhibitory interneurons, reducing C-fibre pain transmission. This explains paresthesia-based SCS. However, HF10-SCS and burst SCS produce equivalent or better analgesia WITHOUT paresthesiae — proving that additional mechanisms (supraspinal, glial, neurochemical) contribute beyond the simple gate model. The gate theory remains a conceptual foundation but does not fully explain modern SCS efficacy.

sections.title: Evidence Base for SCS: PROCESS Trial and FBSS

blocks.content:
The strongest evidence for SCS is in **failed back surgery syndrome (FBSS)** and **complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS)**. FBSS describes persistent or recurrent leg pain and/or back pain after technically adequate lumbar spine surgery — affecting approximately 10-15% of patients undergoing lumbar surgery and representing a major cause of chronic neuropathic pain and disability.

The **PROCESS trial (Kumar et al. Pain 2007)** was the landmark RCT of SCS for FBSS: 100 patients with FBSS (predominant leg pain, previous lumbar surgery) randomised to SCS plus conservative medical management (CMM) vs CMM alone. At 6 months: **SCS group — 48% achieved ≥50% leg pain relief vs 9% in CMM group (P<0.0001)**; at 24 months: 37% SCS vs 2% CMM (P<0.0001). Crossover was permitted — 32 patients crossed to SCS at 6 months, confirming patient preference. VAS leg pain: SCS -50% vs CMM -4%. Quality of life (EQ-5D): significantly better in SCS group. **NICE TA159 (2008)** recommends SCS for FBSS based on PROCESS: SCS is cost-effective at a cost per QALY gained of approximately £19,000 (below the £20,000 NICE threshold).

The **North RCT (NEJM 2005)** compared SCS to repeat lumbar surgery for FBSS: SCS achieved ≥50% pain relief in 47% vs 12% with repeated surgery (P=0.02); fewer complications; lower medication use. This trial established that SCS should precede repeat surgery for FBSS — repeat surgery is rarely justified when SCS has not been tried.

**CRPS (Complex Regional Pain Syndrome)** Types I (no nerve injury) and II (with nerve injury) are other primary indications. The Dutch CRPS trial (Kemler et al. NEJM 2000): 54 CRPS patients randomised to SCS + PT vs PT alone — SCS group: 39% ≥50% pain relief at 6 months vs 0% PT alone (P<0.001). At 2 years: maintained. This RCT provided Level I evidence for SCS in CRPS.

Other established SCS indications: **refractory angina pectoris** (Karolinska protocol — SCS reduces angina episodes and improves exercise tolerance via spinal inhibition of cardiac afferents); **peripheral vascular disease/critical limb ischaemia** (SCS induces vasodilation and increases microvascular flow, delaying amputation).

blocks.title: SCS Candidacy for FBSS — Patient Selection
blocks.content: Ideal FBSS candidate for SCS: (1) predominant LEG pain (>50% leg pain component — back-dominant FBSS responds less well); (2) neuropathic pain quality (burning, shooting, allodynia); (3) failed adequate conservative management ≥6 months (physiotherapy, medications, epidural injections); (4) no untreated depression or substance abuse; (5) no active spinal instability requiring further surgery; (6) positive SCS trial (≥50% relief with temporary percutaneous leads). Back-dominant FBSS with minimal leg pain has lower SCS response rates — consider HF10-SCS which has demonstrated better back pain outcomes than conventional paresthesia-based SCS.

**Intrathecal drug delivery systems (IDDS)** deliver medications directly into the intrathecal space (CSF) via an implanted catheter connected to a subcutaneous programmable pump. The rationale: intrathecal delivery achieves pharmacological effect at the spinal cord dorsal horn with 300-1000× lower drug doses than systemic administration, reducing systemic side effects (sedation, constipation, immunosuppression with opioids) while maximising local analgesic effect. This is particularly valuable for cancer pain and for patients with severe side effects from oral or parenteral opioids.

The **intrathecal analgesic ladder** (Deer et al. Neuromodulation 2017 Polyanalgesic Consensus Conference, PACC) defines first-line agents: **Tier 1**: morphine (FDA-approved) and ziconotide (Prialt, N-type voltage-gated calcium channel blocker, FDA-approved for severe chronic pain). Ziconotide is unique — it is NOT an opioid and can be used in opioid-tolerant patients or those with opioid side effects; narrow therapeutic window (neuropsychiatric side effects: confusion, memory impairment, visual disturbances at too-high doses — requires slow titration). **Tier 2**: hydromorphone (more potent than morphine, similar profile), fentanyl (for opioid-tolerant patients), morphine + bupivacaine (local anaesthetic synergy). **Tier 3**: bupivacaine alone, clonidine (alpha-2 agonist, adjuvant). Baclofen (GABA-B agonist) is used intrathecally specifically for **spasticity** (not pain), and is separately FDA-approved via an IDDS for spastic cerebral palsy, multiple sclerosis, and spinal cord injury spasticity.

**Patient selection for IDDS**: (1) cancer pain — inadequate analgesia or intolerable side effects despite oral/parenteral opioids; life expectancy >3 months (to justify implantation); (2) non-cancer pain — failed multiple conservative and interventional strategies including SCS trial; chronic refractory pain (FBSS, CRPS, failed SCS, ischaemic pain); (3) positive intrathecal trial (direct injection or trial catheter demonstrating ≥50% pain relief). **Smith et al. RCT (JAMA 2002)**: 202 cancer pain patients randomised to IDDS vs comprehensive medical management (CMM) — IDDS group had significantly better pain control (clinical success 85% vs 71%, P=0.05), less toxicity, and survival advantage at 6 months (53.9% vs 37.2% survival) — though the survival advantage may reflect reduced opioid toxicity rather than direct effect.

blocks.title: Intrathecal Baclofen Withdrawal — Life-Threatening Emergency
blocks.content: Abrupt withdrawal of intrathecal baclofen (from pump failure, catheter displacement, or medication error) causes a life-threatening withdrawal syndrome: severe hyperthermia (>40°C), tachycardia, seizures, rhabdomyolysis, multi-organ failure, and death within 24-48 hours. Any patient with an intrathecal baclofen pump presenting with fever, rigidity, seizures, or altered consciousness requires immediate evaluation — check pump function, catheter integrity, and residual drug. Treat with oral or intravenous baclofen, benzodiazepines for spasticity and seizures, and ICU admission. Do NOT delay treatment to confirm pump malfunction.

sections.title: Ablative Procedures: Cordotomy, DREZ, and Rhizotomy

blocks.content:
Ablative (destructive) neurosurgical procedures for pain are generally reserved for patients with severe refractory pain, particularly cancer pain with limited life expectancy, where the irreversibility of the procedure is acceptable. The principle is to interrupt pain transmission pathways at a point proximal to the pain generator but distal to consciousness.

**Percutaneous cordotomy** targets the **spinothalamic tract (STT)** in the anterolateral quadrant of the spinal cord, producing contralateral loss of pain and temperature below the lesion. It is performed via a C1-C2 level anterolateral percutaneous radiofrequency approach (Spencer technique): a radiofrequency electrode is introduced through the C1-C2 disc space under fluoroscopic guidance and positioned in the STT. The electrode position is confirmed by electrical stimulation (concordant paraesthesiae in the pain distribution); a lesion is then created with radiofrequency heat. Cordotomy provides excellent pain relief in 80-90% of patients immediately post-procedure and is particularly effective for **unilateral cancer pain** (chest wall, lung, brachial plexus invasion, pelvic cancer on one side) — however, bilateral cordotomy at high cervical levels risks **Ondine's curse** (loss of automatic ventilatory drive during sleep — the anterolateral cord also contains the reticulospinal pathway mediating sleep-related breathing). Repeat cordotomy for contralateral pain is possible on the same side or opposite side with caution. Duration of effect: 6-12 months (STT fibres may regenerate).

**Dorsal Root Entry Zone (DREZ) lesioning** addresses the DREZ region (where dorsal root fibres enter the spinal cord and synapse in the substantia gelatinosa laminae I-V). Radiofrequency lesions are made in the DREZ at multiple levels, destroying the peripheral pain input transmission zone. Indications: **brachial plexus avulsion pain** (most established indication — pain following motorcycle accident with C5-T1 avulsion; 75-80% significant relief at 12 months) and **post-spinal cord injury pain** (dysesthetic pain below level). DREZ creates ipsilateral analgesia and temperature loss in the affected dermatomes with preservation of motor function (ventral horn preserved).

**Radiofrequency rhizotomy** — targeted destruction of individual dorsal root ganglia or root fascicles for radicular pain. **Cervical medial branch rhizotomy** (facet joint denervation) for facetogenic cervical spondylosis pain. **Trigeminal percutaneous rhizotomy** is addressed in the next chapter (D4). **Cingulotomy** (radiofrequency or radiosurgery lesion of the anterior cingulate cortex) modulates the affective/emotional dimension of pain — reduces pain suffering and suffering without affecting sensory pain intensity. Used for cancer pain with significant psychological distress component, or in intractable chronic pain in carefully selected patients.

blocks.title: Brachial Plexus Avulsion — DREZ Is the Gold Standard
blocks.content: Brachial plexus avulsion injury (classically from motorcycle accidents — C5-T1 deafferentation) causes severe, constant, burning dysesthetic pain in the deafferented arm. This deafferentation pain is NOT amenable to SCS (no peripheral nerve input to stimulate) and responds poorly to opioids. DREZ lesioning of C4-T2 levels provides >75% significant relief at 12 months — the best pain outcome of any procedure for this refractory condition. The mechanism: DREZ destroys the hyperactive dorsal horn neurons that fire spontaneously after deafferentation (deafferentation hyperalgesia).

sections.title: High-Frequency SCS and Dorsal Root Ganglion Stimulation

blocks.content:
**High-Frequency SCS at 10 kHz (HF10-SCS)** represents a major advance in SCS technology. Traditional paresthesia-based SCS requires the patient to feel paresthesiae overlapping the pain area — this demands careful electrode positioning to achieve overlap, is posture-dependent (paresthesiae intensity changes with body position), and some patients find paresthesiae uncomfortable. HF10-SCS operates at 10,000 Hz (vs 40-100 Hz conventional) with very short pulse widths — at these parameters, no paresthesiae are generated (sub-threshold for sensory neuron activation but above threshold for dorsal horn modulation through mechanisms that are not fully understood, potentially involving dorsal horn glial modulation and descending pathway activation). The **SENZA-RCT (Kapural et al. Anesthesiology 2015)**: 198 FBSS patients randomised to HF10-SCS vs conventional SCS. Primary outcome: HF10-SCS superior for BACK pain (84% responders vs 44% conventional, P<0.001); equivalent for leg pain (79% vs 75%). This established HF10-SCS as preferred for back-dominant FBSS.

**Dorsal Root Ganglion (DRG) stimulation** targets the DRG within the neural foramen — the DRG contains the cell bodies of primary afferent sensory neurons and is a key locus of peripheral sensitisation. DRG stimulation provides highly selective dermatomal coverage with less current spread to areas outside the pain distribution, allowing treatment of difficult anatomical locations: foot pain (L5-S1 DRG — poorly covered by conventional epidural SCS), groin/inguinal pain (L1-L2 DRG), chest wall pain, and **CRPS Type I** with foot involvement. The ACCURATE trial (Deer et al. JAMA Neurology 2017): 152 CRPS patients randomised to DRG stimulation vs SCS — DRG superior at 3 months (81.2% success vs 55.7%, P<0.001) and 12 months (74.2% vs 53.0%); DRG also associated with fewer positional paresthesia changes and less side-effects outside the pain area. DRG stimulation is now FDA-approved for CRPS and focal neuropathic pain.

blocks.title: Choosing the SCS Modality
blocks.content: Conventional SCS: established evidence, FBSS with leg predominance, CRPS. HF10-SCS: FBSS with back pain component (back AND leg); paresthesia-intolerant patients; SENZA-RCT showing superiority for back pain. Burst SCS: theoretical advantage for affective/emotional pain dimension; paresthesia-free; no superior evidence over HF10 currently. DRG stimulation: focal neuropathic pain, CRPS with foot/leg/groin distribution, post-surgical neuropathic pain in difficult anatomical zones. In practice, most modern centres offer HF10 or burst as default options, reserving DRG for specific anatomical indications.

sections.title: Complications and Long-Term Outcomes of Implantable Pain Devices

blocks.content:
Complications of spinal cord stimulation and IDDS can be classified as surgical, hardware-related, and biological. **SCS complications**: Lead migration — the most common hardware problem (13-23% of implanted patients in major series), causing loss of paresthesia coverage and efficacy; detected by reduced or altered paresthesia distribution; managed by fluoroscopic repositioning or replacement. Infection — 3-5% of implanted sites; superficial infections treated with antibiotics; deep infections require device explantation (cannot leave infected hardware in epidural space — risk of epidural abscess and meningitis). Dural puncture during lead placement — 0.3-1%; causes post-dural puncture headache; manage with blood patch if conservative treatment fails. Lead fracture — 1-3% per year.

**IDDS complications**: Granuloma formation at catheter tip — a fibrotic inflammatory mass developing at the catheter orifice in the intrathecal space; incidence ~3% of long-term IDDS patients; more common with morphine (less with hydromorphone) and with high concentrations; causes progressive increasing dose requirements and worsening pain despite dose escalation — paradoxical pain worsening should prompt catheter tip MRI. Infection (meningitis, pocket infection) — 2-4%; requires urgent device explantation. Pump malfunction — rare but serious; untreated pump failure = opioid or baclofen withdrawal (potentially life-threatening). Programming errors (incorrect drug, dose calculation errors) — cause opioid overdose or underdosing; strict protocols for refills.

**Long-term SCS outcomes**: Initial responder rates (≥50% relief at trial) are maintained in 60-70% of patients at 2 years; at 5 years, 50-60% remain responders. Habituation (progressive reduction in efficacy despite continued stimulation) affects ~20-30% of long-term SCS patients — managed by reprogramming, changing stimulation paradigm (conventional to burst/HF), or lead repositioning. The PROCESS trial 24-month data showed 37% responders — demonstrating sustained (though diminished) benefit over time. When SCS efficacy is lost and medical management is optimised, intrathecal drug delivery can be the next escalation step.
